one_line_takeaway: A framework-and-taxonomy survey that introduces a three-layer architecture for networked multi-agent semantic communication (SemComNet) and, for the first time in this subfield, systematically enumerates 21 layer-specific security/privacy threats and their countermeasures.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is a gap-and-framework result rather than an empirical one: networked, multi-agent SemCom (SemComNet) is largely unexplored and its security/privacy has been overlooked by prior surveys, and the proposed three-layer architecture provides a structure under which threats and defenses can be systematically enumerated. Concretely, the survey shows each layer introduces distinctive, non-traditional attack surfaces -- e.g., shared knowledge bases enable poisoning/desynchronization/mismatch and model-IP theft at the control layer; the semantic (rather than bit) transmission introduces semantic adversarial/jamming/eavesdropping and malicious-relay risks; and the cognitive layer adds cognitive manipulation, false data injection, and trust/impersonation risks -- and it maps each to existing or candidate countermeasures, concluding with open research directions toward intelligent, robust, energy-efficient SemComNet.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an experimental paper) that provides what the authors claim is the first comprehensive analysis of Semantic Communication Networks (SemComNet) for multi-agent systems, centered on their fundamentals, security, and privacy. Its two distinctive own contributions, which it explicitly positions against prior SemCom surveys, are: (1) a novel three-layer functional architecture for multi-agent semantic interaction -- a control layer (KB maintenance, task scheduling, resource coordination across cloud-edge-end), a semantic transmission layer (semantic-oriented information delivery), and a cognitive sensing layer (environment sensing, agent intent inference, structuring data into contextual knowledge); and (2) a layer-by-layer taxonomy of 21 security/privacy threats plus a five-category catalog of state-of-the-art countermeasures. The authors are careful in their Related Works to distinguish their scope from prior surveys (Lan et al. [27], Shi et al. [13], Luo et al. [2], Qin et al. [28], Uysal et al. [29], Zhang et al. [12], Gunduz et al. [30], Yang et al. [31], Lu et al. [32], Trevlakis et al. [33]), arguing those either treat only paired two-agent SemCom or, when they do cover networked SemCom, omit working modes, use cases, and -- critically -- security and privacy. The three-layer architecture is stated to be built 'based on previous studies [13],[26],[41],' so it is a synthesis rather than a wholly de novo construct; the paper's genuine novelty is the networked framing plus the security/privacy taxonomy.
threat_model: The survey frames adversaries around the classic CIA triad applied to SemComNet resources (raw data, shared knowledge bases, and AI-driven semantic models). Confidentiality threats: unauthorized access to sensitive resources and privacy/intellectual-property infringement (e.g., semantic eavesdropping, unauthorized KB access, model theft/IP infringement). Integrity threats: attacks that alter or corrupt data/knowledge -- false data injection, KB poisoning, data/model poisoning, and adversarial (semantic) attacks that distort semantic meaning. Availability threats: service-disruption attacks such as DoS via sponge examples, semantic jamming, and malware. The taxonomy is organized by which of the three layers is targeted: Control layer (8 threats T1.1-T1.8: sponge examples, KB poisoning, KB desynchronization, KB mismatch, unauthorized KB access, IP infringement of shared models, query misguiding, resource-allocation threats); Semantic transmission layer (7 threats T2.1-T2.7: semantic data poisoning, semantic model poisoning, semantic adversarial, semantic jamming, semantic eavesdropping, malicious semantic relay, feedback exploitation); Cognitive sensing layer (6 threats T3.1-T3.6: cognitive manipulation, false data injection, free-riding, impersonation, trust-management threats, malware).
limitations: As a survey it presents no original experiments, benchmarks, or quantitative validation of the proposed architecture or threat taxonomy; the taxonomy's completeness and the three-layer decomposition are asserted analytically rather than empirically tested. The architecture is a synthesis of prior work ([13],[26],[41]), so its novelty lies mainly in the networked-plus-security framing. Many listed threats are extrapolated from adjacent domains (traditional DL/network security) into the SemComNet setting rather than demonstrated against real SemComNet systems, and defenses are surveyed at a conceptual level with noted individual weaknesses (e.g., fine-pruning scales poorly and needs clean data; Krum's cost grows with participant count). Note for grounding: the paper covered the first 22 PDF pages, which include the full architecture, working modes, complete threat taxonomy (T1.1-T3.6), and countermeasure categories A-C; the later countermeasure subsections (trust management, data & knowledge security), future-directions, and conclusion sections fell just beyond the extraction window and are summarized from the abstract/intro framing rather than their full text.

quantitative_results: Prediction cited from Research&Market [1]: over 125 billion AI devices (agents) connected to the Internet by 2030
21 security/privacy threats enumerated across three layers: 8 control-layer (T1.1-T1.8), 7 semantic-transmission-layer (T2.1-T2.7), 6 cognitive-sensing-layer (T3.1-T3.6)
5 countermeasure categories: semantic model security, semantic transmission security, reliable SemComNet, trust management, data & knowledge security
3 proposed architectural layers (control, semantic transmission, cognitive sensing) and 3 working modes (paired, clustered, networked SemCom)
99 references cited (survey; no experimental metrics of the authors' own)
baselines_compared: Prior SemCom surveys contrasted in Table II: Luo et al. [2], Zhang et al. [12], Shi et al. [13], Lan et al. [27], Qin et al. [28], Uysal et al. [29], Gunduz et al. [30], Yang et al. [31], Lu et al. [32], Trevlakis et al. [33] -- the paper argues these focus on paired SemCom or omit working modes/use cases/security-privacy
